questionText: Before marrying Dana, Lee, a member of a fraternal insurance association, orally told Dana that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate. The promise was discussed informally before the wedding. Dana married Lee. After the marriage, Lee filed the association's standard form naming Dana as beneficiary. The association's by-laws allowed a member to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and several years afterward Lee followed that procedure and named his two children from an earlier marriage as the new beneficiaries. When Lee died, Dana claimed the proceeds based on Lee's pre-marriage promise and his earlier designation of her after the wedding. The children claimed the proceeds under the later beneficiary change. Who has the better claim to the insurance proceeds? Analyze.

responses.responseText:
The children likely have the better claim.

The key issues are:

1. whether Lee’s premarital oral promise to Dana created an enforceable contractual right to the insurance proceeds; and  
2. whether Lee’s post-marriage designation of Dana gave her a vested right that could not later be changed.

On the first issue, Lee’s promise was made before the marriage and was expressly in exchange for Dana’s marrying him. That makes it an antenuptial agreement made upon consideration of marriage. Under the Statute of Frauds, agreements made in consideration of marriage generally must be in writing to be enforceable, other than the mutual promise to marry itself. A promise such as “marry me and I will make you beneficiary of my insurance” is the classic kind of marriage-consideration promise that must be written. Here the promise was only oral and discussed informally before the wedding, so Dana cannot enforce it as a binding contract.

Dana’s marriage does not cure that problem. At common law, the marriage is the consideration for the promise; it does not usually take the agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. So Dana cannot get the proceeds by arguing that Lee was contractually bound, by virtue of the oral premarital agreement, to keep her as beneficiary.

On the second issue, Lee did in fact name Dana as beneficiary after the marriage. But the association’s by-laws expressly allowed him to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and Lee followed that procedure. In fraternal benefit insurance, where the rules reserve to the member the power to change beneficiaries, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property right. So Dana’s designation gave her no irrevocable entitlement unless there was some separate enforceable contract restricting Lee’s power to change it.

There was no such enforceable restriction here. The only alleged restriction was the oral premarital promise, and that fails under the Statute of Frauds. Therefore Lee remained free under the by-laws to substitute his children, and his later change was effective.

As a result, the children, as the beneficiaries named in the valid later change, are entitled to the proceeds.

If Lee had made the promise in a valid written antenuptial agreement, Dana might have been able to argue that the children held the proceeds subject to a constructive trust in her favor. But on these facts, because the promise was oral and the policy was freely changeable, Dana’s claim fails.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage statement to Dana was an oral antenuptial promise: if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary. A promise made upon consideration of marriage is generally within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable. Because this promise was only oral and discussed informally before the wedding, Dana cannot enforce it as a binding contract requiring Lee to keep her as beneficiary.

Dana’s marriage does not cure that problem. Even though she married Lee in reliance on the promise, the agreement is still one made in consideration of marriage, and the usual rule is that such agreements must be written.

That leaves Lee’s later act, after the marriage, of naming Dana as beneficiary on the association’s standard form. But under the association’s by-laws, Lee retained the right to change beneficiaries by filing the required sworn request. Where the insured reserves that power, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property right. So Dana’s designation gave her no irrevocable claim to the proceeds.

Several years later, Lee complied with the by-laws and validly changed the beneficiaries to his two children. Since Dana had no enforceable antenuptial contract and no vested right under the earlier designation, the later change controls.

Dana’s strongest argument would be that Lee’s promise plus her marriage created an equitable obligation that should override the later change. That argument would be stronger if there had been a valid written antenuptial agreement, or some separate enforceable contract supported by consideration other than marriage. But on these facts, the underlying promise is barred by the Statute of Frauds, so equity will not use it to defeat the association’s valid later beneficiary designation.

Therefore, the children are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana was an oral promise made in consideration of marriage: “if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary.” A promise made upon consideration of marriage is ordinarily within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable. Because this agreement was only oral and discussed informally before the wedding, Dana cannot enforce it as a binding antenuptial contract.

Lee’s later act of naming Dana as beneficiary after the marriage does not cure that problem. It shows he initially performed the oral promise, but it did not give Dana an irrevocable right unless there was an enforceable contract limiting his power to change beneficiaries.

Under the association’s by-laws, Lee retained the right to change beneficiaries by filing the required sworn request. He later complied with that procedure and designated his children. In fraternal insurance and similar beneficiary arrangements, a named beneficiary generally has only an expectancy, not a vested property right, so long as the member reserves the contractual power to change the designation. Dana therefore had no fixed right in the certificate proceeds merely because she had once been named.

Could Dana still argue equity—that Lee contracted to keep her as beneficiary and that the children should hold the proceeds in constructive trust for her? Only if she could prove an enforceable agreement. But the only contract she relies on is the oral premarital promise, and that is the type of agreement the Statute of Frauds bars.

So:

1. The oral premarital promise is unenforceable because it was made in consideration of marriage and not reduced to writing.
2. Dana’s post-marriage designation as beneficiary gave her only a revocable expectancy, because the by-laws allowed Lee to change beneficiaries.
3. Lee validly changed the beneficiary to his children before death.

Accordingly, the children are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise to Dana is not enough to give her rights to the proceeds after Lee later changed the beneficiary designation.

First, the alleged agreement was made before marriage and was a promise made in consideration of marriage: Dana would marry Lee, and Lee would make her the beneficiary of his insurance. Agreements made upon consideration of marriage generally must be in writing to be enforceable under the Statute of Frauds, unless they are merely mutual promises to marry. This was not just a promise to marry; it was a collateral financial promise tied to the marriage. Because it was only oral, Dana ordinarily cannot enforce it as a contract.

Second, Dana’s marriage to Lee does not cure that problem. Marriage is the very consideration that brings the promise within the Statute of Frauds, so the fact that she performed by marrying him generally does not make the oral agreement enforceable.

Third, Lee’s later act of naming Dana as beneficiary after the marriage did not give her an irrevocable right. In a fraternal insurance association, where the certificate and by-laws permit the member to change beneficiaries, the named beneficiary usually has only an expectancy, not a vested interest, until the insured dies. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and he later complied with that procedure and named his children. Once he validly did that, Dana’s prior designation was revoked.

Dana’s best possible argument is that Lee’s oral promise and his initial post-marriage designation created an equitable right in her, so the children should hold the proceeds in constructive trust for her. But that argument depends on proving an enforceable contract not to change the beneficiary. Because the only such agreement was oral and made in consideration of marriage, it is unenforceable. Without an enforceable contract limiting Lee’s power to change beneficiaries, Dana cannot override the association records.

So, as between Dana and the children, the children are entitled to the proceeds under the valid later beneficiary designation.

The children have the better claim.

Dana’s argument depends on two ideas: first, that Lee’s oral promise before marriage created an enforceable obligation to keep her as beneficiary, and second, that Lee’s post-marriage designation of her gave her a superior right to the proceeds. Both points are weak.

First, Lee’s pre-marriage promise was a promise made in consideration of marriage. A promise such as “if you marry me, I will make you beneficiary of my insurance” is the classic kind of antenuptial agreement that falls within the Statute of Frauds. Unless reduced to writing, it is generally unenforceable. The marriage itself does not usually take such an oral agreement out of the statute. So Dana cannot enforce the oral premarital promise as a binding contract to award her the proceeds.

Second, Dana’s later designation as beneficiary did not give her a vested right if the fraternal association’s rules allowed Lee to change beneficiaries. In fraternal-benefit insurance, the member’s rights and the beneficiary’s rights are controlled by the certificate and the association’s by-laws. Where those by-laws expressly reserve to the member the power to change beneficiaries by a stated procedure, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a fixed property interest, during the member’s life.

Here, the by-laws allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and he later followed that exact procedure and named his children. Because Dana’s status was revocable under the governing rules, Lee’s later change was effective. Once he validly substituted the children, they became entitled to the proceeds at his death.

Dana might try to argue that equity should enforce Lee’s promise because she married him in reliance on it. But that argument runs into the same Statute of Frauds problem: the underlying marriage-based promise was oral. And the fact that Lee initially named her after the marriage does not convert her revocable designation into an irrevocable one. He performed the initial promise by naming her, but under the certificate terms he still retained the contractual power to change the beneficiary later.

So, as between Dana and the children, the children should receive the insurance proceeds. Dana’s premarital oral promise is not enforceable to defeat a later valid beneficiary change, and her earlier designation gave her no vested right against Lee’s reserved power to substitute new beneficiaries.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s premarital promise to Dana is the weak point in her case. A promise made in consideration of marriage—other than the mutual promise to marry itself—generally falls within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable. Lee’s oral statement that he would make Dana the beneficiary if she married him was exactly that kind of collateral marriage promise. Because it was only oral, Dana ordinarily cannot enforce it as a contract.

Dana’s marriage to Lee does not cure that problem. In this setting, the marriage is the consideration for the promise, but the law still generally requires the promise to be in writing. So Dana cannot rely on the premarital oral agreement to claim the proceeds.

Her second argument—that Lee actually named her beneficiary after the wedding—is also not enough. In a fraternal insurance association, where the by-laws reserve to the member the right to change beneficiaries by following a stated procedure, the named beneficiary typically has only an expectancy, not a vested property right, until the member dies. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and he later complied with that procedure and named his children. That later change controls unless Dana had some independent enforceable contractual right preventing Lee from changing the designation.

She did not. The only alleged contract was the oral antenuptial promise, and that is unenforceable. So when Lee later changed the beneficiary in the manner required by the association’s rules, Dana’s expectancy was cut off and the children became entitled to the proceeds.

Thus, the proceeds should go to the children, not Dana. Dana may feel morally wronged, but legally her claim is inferior because:
1. the premarital promise was oral and unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, and
2. her earlier beneficiary designation was revocable under the association’s by-laws and did not vest in her an irrevocable right.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana is likely unenforceable because it was an oral promise made in consideration of marriage. Agreements made upon consideration of marriage ordinarily fall within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing. Here, Lee allegedly said that if Dana married him, he would make her the beneficiary. That is exactly the kind of antenuptial bargain the statute requires to be memorialized in writing. The fact that Dana went through with the marriage does not usually cure the lack of a writing.

Dana’s second argument—that Lee actually did name her beneficiary after the wedding—is also weak because the insurance contract and the association’s by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing the required sworn request. Where the member reserves the right to change the beneficiary, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property right. So when Lee first named Dana, she did not acquire an irrevocable right to the proceeds unless there was some separate enforceable agreement preventing him from changing her designation.

There was no such enforceable agreement here. The only alleged agreement was the oral premarital promise, and that is barred by the Statute of Frauds. Once that promise drops out, Dana is left only with a revocable beneficiary designation. Lee later complied with the association’s by-laws and validly substituted his children. On his death, the children, as the last properly designated beneficiaries, are entitled to the proceeds.

Dana might try to argue equity—that she married in reliance on Lee’s promise and that it would be unfair to let him revoke her designation. But courts generally do not impose a constructive trust in these circumstances when the underlying marriage-based promise is oral and unenforceable, especially in the context of a fraternal benefit certificate that expressly permits beneficiary changes.

So, the proceeds should go to the children.

The children have the better claim.

The key points are:

1. Lee’s promise to Dana before the marriage was oral and was made in consideration of marriage.
A promise such as, “If you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary of my insurance,” is an antenuptial agreement. Agreements made upon consideration of marriage generally fall within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable. Because Lee’s promise was only oral, Dana ordinarily cannot enforce it as a binding contract.

2. Dana’s marriage does not cure the lack of a writing.
Courts generally do not treat the marriage itself as sufficient part performance to take an oral antenuptial agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. So Dana cannot rely on the premarital oral promise alone to claim the proceeds.

3. Lee’s later designation of Dana as beneficiary gave her only a revocable expectancy, not a vested right.
The association’s by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. In fraternal insurance, those by-laws are part of the contract. Where the member reserves the right to change the beneficiary, the named beneficiary has no vested interest during the member’s lifetime unless there is some separate enforceable contract making the designation irrevocable.

4. There was no separate enforceable contract making Dana’s designation irrevocable.
Dana’s only contractual theory is Lee’s premarital promise. But that promise is unenforceable because it was oral and made in consideration of marriage. So when Lee named Dana after the wedding, she became beneficiary only subject to his reserved power to change the designation later.

5. Lee properly exercised that reserved power.
The facts say Lee later followed the association’s prescribed procedure and named his two children as the new beneficiaries. Since Dana had no vested or irrevocable right, that change was effective.

So Dana’s claim fails both ways:
- the oral premarital promise is unenforceable, and
- her earlier beneficiary designation was revocable under the certificate and by-laws.

Accordingly, the proceeds belong to the children, as the validly substituted beneficiaries at Lee’s death.

The children have the better claim.

Dana’s argument has two possible bases: (1) Lee’s oral premarital promise that he would make her beneficiary if she married him, and (2) Lee’s actual post-marriage designation of her as beneficiary. Neither is enough to defeat the later valid change to the children.

First, the premarital promise is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. A promise made “in consideration of marriage” must generally be in writing, other than the mutual promise to marry itself. Lee’s statement was exactly that: if Dana married him, he would make her beneficiary. Because that was an oral antenuptial promise induced by marriage, Dana cannot enforce it as a binding contract after Lee’s death. Her marriage to Lee does not usually take that kind of agreement out of the statute.

Second, Dana did become the named beneficiary after the wedding, but that did not give her a vested right to the proceeds. In a fraternal insurance association, the certificate and the association’s by-laws control. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. He did so in the prescribed manner, naming his children. Where the member reserves and properly exercises the power to change beneficiaries, the earlier beneficiary generally has only an expectancy, not a fixed property interest.

Dana would have a stronger case if there had been a valid, enforceable contract obligating Lee to keep her as beneficiary, because a court can in some circumstances impose a constructive trust on proceeds paid to later beneficiaries. But that theory fails here because the only alleged contract was the oral promise made in consideration of marriage, which is unenforceable.

So, because Lee validly changed the beneficiary under the association’s rules, and because Dana cannot enforce the earlier oral marriage-based promise, the proceeds should go to the children.

The children have the better claim.

Dana’s case rests on two points: Lee’s oral promise before marriage, and Lee’s later post-marriage designation of her as beneficiary. Neither is enough to defeat the children’s later valid designation.

First, Lee’s pre-marriage promise is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. A promise made “in consideration of marriage” — meaning a collateral promise induced by the marriage, not merely the mutual promise to marry — generally must be in writing. Lee’s alleged promise was exactly that: “if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary.” Because it was oral, Dana ordinarily cannot enforce it as a binding antenuptial contract. The fact that Dana actually married Lee does not usually save such an oral agreement.

Second, Dana’s designation as beneficiary after the marriage did not give her a vested right to the proceeds. In a fraternal benefit or mutual insurance certificate, where the by-laws expressly allow the member to change beneficiaries, the named beneficiary generally has only an expectancy until the member dies. Here, the association’s by-laws expressly permitted Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and Lee complied with that procedure. Once he validly changed the designation to his children, Dana’s prior expectancy was cut off.

Dana would need some independent legal basis to prevent Lee from exercising the reserved right to change beneficiaries — for example, a valid enforceable contract in which Lee gave up that right. But the only contract she can point to is the oral premarital promise, and that is the very agreement likely barred by the Statute of Frauds. So she cannot turn her prior designation into an irrevocable one.

In short:

- the oral antenuptial promise is unenforceable;
- the first beneficiary designation gave Dana no vested rights because the by-laws allowed change;
- Lee later changed the beneficiary in the manner required by the association.

Therefore, the children, as the later properly designated beneficiaries, are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children likely have the better claim.

The key points are:

1. Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana was oral and made in consideration of marriage.
A promise such as “if you marry me, I will make you beneficiary” is an antenuptial agreement made upon consideration of marriage. Agreements of that kind generally fall within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable, unless they are simply the mutual promise to marry. This was not merely a promise to marry; it was a collateral promise about property rights. So Dana cannot enforce that oral promise as a binding contract.

2. Dana’s marriage to Lee does not cure the Statute of Frauds problem.
Dana would argue that she performed by marrying Lee. But the whole point of the statute is that a property promise made in consideration of marriage must be evidenced by writing. Marriage, standing alone, ordinarily does not take such an agreement out of the statute. So Dana cannot rely on the premarital oral promise to freeze her status as beneficiary.

3. Dana’s post-marriage designation as beneficiary did not give her a vested right.
After the wedding, Lee did name Dana as beneficiary on the association’s form. But the association’s by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing the required sworn request. In fraternal insurance, the by-laws are part of the contract. Where the member retains the contractual power to change the beneficiary, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property interest, until the member’s death.

4. Lee validly exercised his reserved right to change the beneficiary.
Several years later, Lee followed the association’s prescribed procedure and changed the designation to his children. Because he complied with the by-laws, that later change was effective unless Dana had some independent enforceable contractual right preventing it. She did not, because the only alleged contract was the unenforceable oral antenuptial promise.

5. No equitable basis is strong enough to defeat the children’s designation.
Courts sometimes impose a constructive trust when an insured changes a beneficiary in violation of a valid contract, such as a written antenuptial or separation agreement. But here there was no valid written agreement. Without such an enforceable obligation, equity usually will not override the association’s valid beneficiary change.

So Dana’s two arguments both fail:
- the premarital oral promise is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds; and
- her earlier designation as beneficiary was revocable under the by-laws and therefore gave her no vested entitlement.

Accordingly, the children, as the later properly designated beneficiaries, have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Dana’s argument depends on two possible theories, and both are weak here.

First, Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise is likely unenforceable. A promise made in consideration of marriage—other than the mutual promise to marry—falls within the Statute of Frauds and generally must be in writing. Lee’s statement that he would make Dana the beneficiary if she married him was exactly that kind of antenuptial promise: a promise to confer a financial benefit in return for marriage. Because it was only oral and discussed informally before the wedding, Dana ordinarily cannot enforce it as a binding contract.

Second, Dana cannot rely on the fact that Lee did in fact name her beneficiary after the marriage. In a fraternal insurance association, the member’s rights are governed by the certificate and the association’s by-laws. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and he complied with that procedure. Where the member reserves the right to change beneficiaries, the originally named beneficiary ordinarily has no vested right during the member’s lifetime—only a revocable expectancy. So Dana’s designation gave her no fixed entitlement that would defeat a later valid change.

By contrast, the children were named through the exact method the by-laws required, and that designation was in effect at Lee’s death. Since beneficiary rights generally become fixed at death, the proceeds belong to the beneficiaries then properly on file unless some superior equitable right exists.

Dana would need such a superior equitable right to win—typically, an enforceable antenuptial agreement that Lee would keep her as beneficiary. If that agreement had been valid and provable, a court might impose a constructive trust on the proceeds despite the later change. But because the promise here was oral and made in consideration of marriage, it is generally barred by the Statute of Frauds. Without an enforceable contract, Dana has only the revoked earlier designation, which is not enough.

So the likely result is that the children, as the later validly designated beneficiaries, are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children likely have the better claim.

The starting point is the nature of Dana’s rights under the fraternal insurance certificate. In a fraternal benefit association, the member’s rights and the beneficiary’s rights are governed by the certificate together with the association’s by-laws. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and Lee complied with that procedure. So, unless Dana can show some separate enforceable agreement that made her designation irrevocable, the later designation of the children controls.

Dana’s main argument is that Lee promised before marriage that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary. The problem is that this was an oral antenuptial promise made in consideration of marriage. Agreements made upon consideration of marriage, other than the mutual promise to marry, are generally within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable. An oral promise of “marry me and I will make you beneficiary” is the classic kind of promise the statute reaches. So Dana cannot enforce that promise as a binding contract unless there is a sufficient writing memorializing it.

The later beneficiary form naming Dana does not likely cure that defect. That form shows only that Lee at that time designated Dana as beneficiary. It does not show an agreement with Dana that the designation was irrevocable, nor does it memorialize the alleged premarital bargain in consideration of marriage. At most, it shows Lee performed the promise for a time. But under the association’s by-laws, that designation remained revocable.

That matters because, in this setting, a named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested right, where the member has reserved the right to change beneficiaries. Dana therefore acquired no fixed entitlement merely because Lee named her after the wedding. Her interest continued only until Lee validly changed the designation. When Lee later followed the association’s prescribed method and named his children, that later change superseded Dana’s earlier status.

Dana might argue equity: she married Lee in reliance on his promise, and it would be unfair to let him defeat it. But courts generally do not let reliance in the form of marriage alone avoid the Statute of Frauds for marriage-consideration contracts, because that would substantially nullify the statute. And absent an enforceable contract, there is no basis to impose a constructive trust on the proceeds in her favor.

So the analysis is:

1. The children are the last duly designated beneficiaries under the association’s rules.
2. Dana’s earlier designation was revocable and gave her only an expectancy.
3. Her premarital oral promise claim is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds because it was an agreement in consideration of marriage and not in writing.
4. Therefore, Lee’s later change to the children is effective, and the proceeds should go to them.

Conclusion: the children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Dana’s argument has two possible foundations, and both are weak:

1. Lee’s oral premarital promise to make her beneficiary if she married him.
2. Lee’s later post-marriage act of naming her as beneficiary.

Neither is enough to defeat the children’s later designation.

First, the premarital promise is likely unenforceable. A promise made “if you marry me, I will provide for you in this way” is an agreement made upon consideration of marriage. Such agreements ordinarily fall within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable. Here, the promise was only oral and was discussed informally before the wedding. Dana’s marriage to Lee does not usually take that kind of agreement out of the statute; marriage is the contemplated consideration, not the written memorandum required by law. So Dana likely cannot enforce the premarital promise as a binding contract.

Second, Dana did not acquire a vested right merely because Lee, after the marriage, filed the association’s form naming her beneficiary. In a fraternal benefit or insurance certificate, the beneficiary’s rights are governed by the certificate and the association’s by-laws. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. Because Lee reserved and exercised that power, Dana’s interest as the earlier beneficiary was only an expectancy, not a vested entitlement. When Lee later complied with the prescribed procedure and substituted his children, that change was effective.

Dana might try to argue that the earlier designation after marriage was performance of the oral premarital agreement and therefore should be protected in equity. But that argument still depends on the existence of an enforceable underlying contract. Since the promise was oral and within the Statute of Frauds, equity is unlikely to impose a constructive trust on the proceeds for her benefit. And absent such an enforceable agreement, the designation of Dana was revocable under the by-laws.

So, at Lee’s death, the valid beneficiary designation on file was the later one naming the children. They therefore have the superior claim to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

The key points are:

1. Under a fraternal insurance certificate, a named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property right, if the association’s by-laws let the member change beneficiaries.
2. Dana can defeat the later change only if Lee had made an enforceable contract giving her a fixed right to the proceeds.

Here, Dana’s main contract theory is Lee’s oral pre-marriage promise that, if she married him, he would make her beneficiary. That is a promise made in consideration of marriage. Such agreements are generally within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable. Because the promise was only oral and informal, Dana usually cannot enforce it as a binding antenuptial contract.

Her marriage to Lee does not solve that problem. Marriage may be the consideration for the promise, but it does not ordinarily take an oral marriage-based agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. So the pre-marriage promise did not give Dana an enforceable vested right in the certificate.

Lee did later name Dana as beneficiary after the wedding. But that act gave her only the rights of a beneficiary under the certificate as governed by the association’s rules. Since the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, Dana’s interest remained revocable. She had no fixed entitlement unless there was a separate enforceable contract restricting Lee’s power to change the designation. On these facts, there was not.

Lee then complied with the by-laws and validly changed the beneficiaries to his children. Because the change was made in the manner required by the association, and because Dana had no enforceable superior equity, the children’s designation controls.

So, on these facts, the proceeds should go to the children, not Dana. Dana’s oral antenuptial agreement is unenforceable, and her earlier designation was subject to Lee’s reserved power to revoke.

The children have the better claim.

Dana’s best argument is that Lee promised before marriage that, if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary, and that he later did name her. But that argument fails for two separate reasons.

First, Lee’s pre-marriage promise was oral and was made in consideration of marriage. A promise to convey property or confer a financial benefit in return for marriage is ordinarily within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable. Dana’s marriage to Lee supplies the consideration, but it does not cure the lack of a writing. Courts generally do not treat the marriage itself as sufficient part performance to take such an agreement out of the statute, because that would defeat the statute’s specific requirement for marriage-based agreements. So Dana cannot enforce the oral antenuptial promise as a binding contract.

Second, even after Lee named Dana as beneficiary, that designation did not give her a vested right if the association’s governing documents allowed Lee to change beneficiaries. In fraternal insurance certificates, the member’s rights are controlled by the association’s charter and by-laws. Where, as here, the by-laws expressly permit a change of beneficiary by filing the required request, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy during the member’s life, not an irrevocable property interest. Lee later complied with the prescribed procedure and designated his children. That later change therefore superseded Dana’s earlier designation.

Dana could prevail only if she could show some independent enforceable agreement obligating Lee to keep her as beneficiary despite his reserved power to change. But the only such agreement here is the oral pre-marital promise, and that is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds.

So, assuming the children were eligible beneficiaries under the association’s rules and Lee properly filed the change, the proceeds belong to the children, not Dana.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage statement to Dana was, in substance, an antenuptial promise: “If you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary.” A promise made in consideration of marriage falls within the Statute of Frauds and generally must be in writing to be enforceable. Because Lee’s promise was only oral and was discussed informally before the wedding, Dana cannot enforce that promise as a contract after Lee’s death. The fact that she did marry him does not usually remove such an agreement from the Statute of Frauds; marriage is the very consideration that triggers the writing requirement.

Dana’s second argument is that Lee did in fact name her beneficiary after the marriage. But that does not give her an irrevocable right if the insurance contract and the association’s by-laws reserved to Lee the power to change beneficiaries. In a fraternal insurance association, the certificate is governed by the association’s rules, and where those rules allow the member to substitute beneficiaries by following a stated procedure, the original beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property interest, until the member dies.

Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and Lee complied with that procedure several years afterward by naming his two children. Once he validly exercised that reserved power, Dana’s earlier designation was revoked.

Dana would prevail only if she could show some enforceable agreement that made her beneficiary designation irrevocable, so that equity should impose a constructive trust on the proceeds despite the later change. But her only alleged agreement is the oral premarital promise, and that is precisely the kind of promise the Statute of Frauds bars. Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana is the weak point in Dana’s case. An oral promise made in consideration of marriage is generally unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. This was not merely a casual promise to give a gift sometime later; it was a promise that Dana says induced her to marry him. Contracts “upon consideration of marriage” ordinarily must be in writing. The fact that Dana did marry Lee does not usually cure the problem, because marriage is the very consideration the statute addresses.

Dana’s second argument—that Lee did in fact name her beneficiary after the marriage—is also not enough by itself. In a fraternal benefit or insurance certificate where the association’s by-laws expressly reserve to the member the right to change beneficiaries, the first-named beneficiary usually acquires only an expectancy, not a vested property right. So long as Lee complied with the by-laws for changing the beneficiary, he could revoke Dana’s designation and substitute someone else.

Could Dana argue that the original designation after marriage was part performance of Lee’s promise and therefore should be protected in equity? Courts sometimes protect a beneficiary named pursuant to a valid contract for value, such as a binding antenuptial agreement, and may impose a constructive trust against later beneficiaries who took only as volunteers. But here the underlying antenuptial promise was oral and therefore likely unenforceable. Without a valid enforceable contract, Dana cannot convert her revocable beneficiary status into a vested equitable right.

- The oral pre-marriage promise is likely barred by the Statute of Frauds.
- Dana’s post-marriage designation gave her only a revocable expectancy because the by-laws let Lee change beneficiaries.
- Lee validly exercised that reserved power and named his children.
- Therefore, the children are entitled to the proceeds.

In short, Dana would probably lose, and the children would take the insurance proceeds.

This was not just a mutual promise to marry; it was a collateral promise to confer a property benefit if Dana married him.

Because the promise was oral, Dana ordinarily cannot enforce it as a contract.

Marriage itself usually does not take such an oral agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. So Dana cannot successfully argue that by marrying Lee she fully performed and thereby made the oral promise enforceable. Courts generally refuse to enforce oral antenuptial property promises of this kind for that reason.

The earlier designation of Dana after the wedding

Lee did, after marriage, file the association’s form naming Dana as beneficiary. That helped Dana only so long as that designation remained in force.

But the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. In fraternal insurance associations, where the member reserves the contractual right to change the beneficiary, the named beneficiary generally has only an expectancy, not a vested property interest, until the member dies without making a valid change.

So Dana’s status as the first named beneficiary did not give her an irrevocable right to the proceeds. Lee remained free to substitute someone else unless he had bound himself by an enforceable agreement not to do so.

Whether Lee had contracted away his right to change

Sometimes a beneficiary can prevail over a later substituted beneficiary if the insured made an enforceable contract, for value, to keep that person as beneficiary. Dana would try to fit within that theory by saying she married Lee in reliance on his promise.

But that argument fails here because the alleged contract was oral and made in consideration of marriage. Since it was not in writing, it is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. Therefore, Lee did not effectively contract away his reserved right to change the beneficiary.

Also, on these facts, Lee literally performed his oral promise to “make” Dana the beneficiary by naming her after the marriage. Nothing in the facts shows a written promise that she would remain beneficiary irrevocably until his death. The by-laws, by contrast, clearly contemplated later change.

The later change to the children

Several years later Lee followed the association’s prescribed procedure and filed the sworn request naming his children as beneficiaries. Because he had reserved the right to change beneficiaries and had not given Dana any enforceable vested contractual right to remain beneficiary, that later change was effective.

Once Lee died, the children were the valid beneficiaries under the certificate then in force.

Conclusion

Dana cannot recover on the basis of the oral premarital promise, because a promise made in consideration of marriage must be in writing to be enforceable. And her earlier designation as beneficiary gave her only a defeasible expectancy, since Lee retained the contractual right to change beneficiaries under the association’s by-laws. Lee validly exercised that right in favor of his children. Therefore, the children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.
